stories.title: Raphael Collignon Wins First ATP Title at Gstaad
stories.angle: NEW STORY — Raphael Collignon (BEL, career high No. 42) defeated No. 3 seed Stefanos Tsitsipas 7-6(2), 7-6(5) in the EFG Swiss Open Gstaad final to claim his first ATP Tour title. Collignon reached the final by saving a match point against Juan Manuel Cerundolo in the SF (came back from 1-6, 3-5 down to win 1-6, 7-6(5), 7-5). Only the second Belgian ever to reach the Gstaad final, after David Goffin in 2015. Tsitsipas was returning to his first ATP final in 17 months.

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Raphael Collignon wins the EFG Swiss Open Gstaad. First ATP title. Belgian beats No. 3 seed Tsitsipas 7-6(2), 7-6(5).

Collignon saved a match point vs. Cerundolo in the SF just to reach this final. 🏆

#TennisFanrecap #ATPTour #Gstaad #SwissOpen

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Tsitsipas waited 17 months for another ATP final. Collignon made it his first — and his last chance didn't slip away.

Two tiebreaks. No third set. Collignon delivered when it mattered. 🎾

#TennisFanrecap #ATPTour #Gstaad #SwissOpen

facebook.long_form:
Raphael Collignon is an ATP Tour champion. The Belgian walked off the clay in Gstaad on Sunday with his first career title, defeating No. 3 seed Stefanos Tsitsipas 7-6(2), 7-6(5) in the EFG Swiss Open final.

Getting to that final required something most players never have to do. Collignon faced match point against Juan Manuel Cerundolo in the semifinals — down a set and 3-5 — and turned it around to win 1-6, 7-6(5), 7-5. Then he turned around and beat one of the tour's most experienced finalists in two tiebreaks without looking rattled for a moment.

For Tsitsipas, this was his first ATP final in 17 months. He played well enough to win it. Collignon simply played better.

Collignon reached a career-high ranking of No. 42 earlier this month and is the only second Belgian to reach the Gstaad final since David Goffin in 2015. Sunday, he did what Goffin didn't — he won it.

What do you make of this week's clay-court first-title stories — Merida at Umag earlier in the week, now Collignon at Gstaad?
facebook.image_caption: Collignon beats Tsitsipas in two tiebreaks. First ATP title at Gstaad. Quite a week to be a new champion on clay. 🏆

article.headline: Collignon Beats Tsitsipas in Two Tiebreaks for First ATP Title at Gstaad

stories.title: Sakkari Wins Athens — Historic Home Title
stories.angle: NEW STORY — Maria Sakkari (GRE, No. 4 seed) won the Vanda Pharmaceuticals Athens Open, the first WTA event in Athens in 35 years, defeating No. 1 seed Barbora Krejcikova in the final. Sakkari reached her first WTA final in over two years (last: Indian Wells runner-up, March 2024). On the same day, Tsitsipas played the Gstaad final — making July 19 a historic day for Greek tennis with two Greek players in ATP/WTA finals simultaneously. SF results: Sakkari def. Korneeva 6-1, 7-5; Krejcikova def. Tauson 6-1, 4-6, 6-3.

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Maria Sakkari wins the Athens Open. First WTA title in Athens in 35 years. At home. In front of her crowd.

Today: Sakkari won in Athens. Tsitsipas played the Gstaad final. Greece had two players in ATP/WTA finals at the same time. 🇬🇷

#TennisFanrecap #WTATour #Athens #AthensOpen

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Sakkari's last WTA final was Indian Wells, March 2024. She waited more than two years to stand on that stage again.

She chose Athens for her return. It doesn't get more fitting than that. 🎾

#TennisFanrecap #WTATour #Athens #AthensOpen

facebook.long_form:
Sunday, July 19 may be the best day in recent Greek tennis history.

Maria Sakkari won the Vanda Pharmaceuticals Athens Open, defeating No. 1 seed Barbora Krejcikova in the final of the first WTA event in Athens in 35 years. Playing in front of her home crowd, Sakkari ended a drought that had lasted more than two years — her last WTA final was Indian Wells in March 2024. She's been close. Today she got there.

At the same time Sakkari was winning in Athens, Stefanos Tsitsipas was in the Gstaad final against Collignon. Two Greek players competing in ATP and WTA finals simultaneously. That does not happen very often, and it happened in Athens for the first time in decades.

Krejcikova, the 2021 Roland Garros champion, reached the Athens final via a comeback win of her own over Clara Tauson (6-1, 4-6, 6-3 in the SF). She played well this week. But the crowd — and the moment — belonged to Sakkari.

Were you watching the Athens final today? What a stage for Greek tennis to reclaim.
facebook.image_caption: Sakkari wins it in Athens. First WTA title in the city in 35 years. The home crowd finally got what they came for. 🇬🇷

article.headline: Sakkari Wins Athens Open in Historic Home Final — First WTA Title in the City in 35 Years

stories.title: Paula Badosa Wins WTA Iasi — Fifth Career Title
stories.angle: NEW STORY — Paula Badosa (ESP) defeated Mayar Sherif (EGY) in the UniCredit Iasi Open final to claim her fifth career WTA title. Badosa entered on a six-match winning streak and has now never lost a WTA title match (5-0). The win pushes her back toward the top 100 — as high as No. 75. She grew up following Simona Halep and has called Iasi "home." Badosa's SF: def. Tamara Zidansek 3-6, 7-5, 7-6 (came back from set and a break down). Sherif's SF: def. Oliynykova 6-3, 6-1.

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
Paula Badosa wins in Iasi. Fifth career WTA title. She has never lost a title match — 5 finals, 5 trophies.

The comeback to the top 100 is no longer a comeback. It's a return. 🏆

#TennisFanrecap #WTATour #Iasi #Badosa

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
Badosa def. Sherif to win Iasi. She was outside the top 100 this year. Now she's headed back as high as No. 75.

Six consecutive match wins. One title. Iasi felt like home — "I grew up following Simona." 🎾

#TennisFanrecap #WTATour #Iasi #Badosa

facebook.long_form:
Paula Badosa won the UniCredit Iasi Open today, defeating Mayar Sherif in the final to claim her fifth career WTA title. She has now entered five WTA title matches and won five. That is a record worth paying attention to.

This week said a lot about where Badosa is right now. She came back from a set and a break down against Tamara Zidansek in the semifinals, winning 3-6, 7-5, 7-6. She brought a six-match winning streak into Sunday's final. She has been playing through the draw like someone who expected to be there.

The ranking picture makes this win even more meaningful. Badosa spent time outside the top 100 earlier in the year. Her Iasi title moves her back as high as No. 75 in the world — enough to enter the US Open directly.

She also has a personal connection to this tournament. "I grew up following Simona," she said after the match, referencing Simona Halep, Romania's most celebrated player. Winning in Romania, at the same tournament where Halep's story looms large, clearly meant something beyond the ranking points.

What are your expectations for Badosa heading into the North American hardcourt swing?
facebook.image_caption: Badosa wins Iasi. Five finals, five titles. She is 5-0 in WTA title matches. That's a different kind of champion. 🏆

article.headline: Badosa Claims Fifth Career WTA Title at Iasi, Returns to Top 100

stories.title: Bastad Final Set — Rublev vs. Darderi (Defending Champion)
stories.angle: CORRECTION/UPDATE from July 18 — The Nordea Open Bastad final is Andrey Rublev (RUS, No. 1 seed, 2023 champion) vs. Luciano Darderi (ITA, No. 2 seed, 2025 defending champion). Yesterday's pipeline incorrectly previewed the final as Rublev vs. Vallejo — Darderi actually outlasted Vallejo 6-4, 6-7(11), 6-3 in the SF. Rublev def. Tabilo 6-4, 4-6, 6-4. Two former Bastad champions in Sunday's final.

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Bastad final: No. 1 seed Rublev (2023 champion) vs. No. 2 seed Darderi (2025 defending champion).

Two former Bastad winners. One title. Sunday clay-court tennis at its best. 🎾

#TennisFanrecap #ATPTour #Bastad #NordeaOpen

facebook.long_form:
The Nordea Open Bastad final has a storyline that writes itself: two former Bastad champions across the net from each other.

No. 1 seed Andrey Rublev, the 2023 Bastad champion, faces No. 2 seed Luciano Darderi, who won the title in 2025 and is defending it today. Neither player had an easy path through the semifinals. Rublev needed three sets to beat Alejandro Tabilo (6-4, 4-6, 6-4), while Darderi survived a marathon against Adolfo Daniel Vallejo that stretched to a second-set tiebreak that went to 11 games before Darderi finally closed it out 6-4, 6-7(11), 6-3.

Worth noting: the July 18 pipeline previewed this final as Rublev vs. Vallejo. Darderi had other plans, fighting through that punishing second set to earn his place in today's final.

One former champion is going to add a second Bastad title. The other is going home with a runner-up result at the event he used to own.

Who's your pick — Rublev or Darderi for the Bastad title?
facebook.image_caption: Two former Bastad champions in today's final. Rublev (2023) vs. Darderi (2025). Someone's adding a second title in Bastad. 🎾

article.headline: Two Former Bastad Champions Meet in Sunday's Nordea Open Final

stories.title: Canadian Open — 71 of 72 Top ATP Players Confirmed
stories.angle: NEW STORY — Tennis Canada confirmed 71 of the ATP Tour's top 72 players for the National Bank Open in Montreal (Aug. 1–13, 2026). Sinner (No. 1, 2023 Montreal champion) and Djokovic both confirmed. Zverev (Roland Garros champion) in. Wild cards: Monfils and Diallo. Notable absence: Alcaraz (right wrist injury since April, missed French Open and Wimbledon). WTA: 72 of 75 entered for Toronto.

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
71 of the ATP Tour's Top 72 confirmed for the Canadian Open in Montreal (Aug. 1–13).

Sinner, Djokovic, Zverev all in. Alcaraz out (wrist).

Strongest entry list in tournament history. North American summer is loading. 🎾

#TennisFanrecap #ATPTour #CanadianOpen #NationalBankOpen

facebook.long_form:
The clay swing is wrapping up this weekend. What comes next is the North American hardcourt summer — and it starts in Montreal.

Tennis Canada just confirmed 71 of the ATP Tour's top 72 players for the National Bank Open (Aug. 1–13). World No. 1 Jannik Sinner, the 2023 Montreal champion who has not lost a Masters 1000 event all season, is in. Novak Djokovic is in. Alexander Zverev, the Roland Garros champion, is in. Félix Auger-Aliassime is making his ninth home appearance.

The one name missing from the top 72: Carlos Alcaraz. His right wrist injury, which has kept him off the tour since April, has followed him through the French Open, Wimbledon, and now Montreal. His return timeline remains uncertain — every week that passes raises questions about whether he makes the US Open.

On the WTA side, 72 of the top 75 players are set for Toronto on the same week.

"Strongest entry list in tournament history" is how Tennis Canada described the field. With Alcaraz absent and Sinner in perfect form, that's a statement worth watching play out.

Who do you think challenges Sinner for the Montreal title?
facebook.image_caption: Montreal is loading. 71 of 72 top ATP players confirmed. Sinner, Djokovic, Zverev all in. Alcaraz still on the sideline. 🎾

article.headline: Sinner, Djokovic, Zverev Head Record Canadian Open Field — Alcaraz Out Again
